metadata.title: Real-World Population Ratios for Archetype Variables
metadata.description: Statistical distributions based on official U.S. government data sources
sources: U.S. Census Bureau - Current Population Survey (CPS) 2023-2024
Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) - Employment Situation 2024
metadata.notes: Ratios represent U.S. population distributions. Some values are rounded to sum to 100%.

1.name: Income level
1.source: Census Bureau Income Report 2023 - quintile distribution approximation
1.notes: Bottom 40% = very low + low, middle 40%, top 20% = high + very high

2.name: Wealth level
2.source: Federal Reserve DFA Q3 2025 - wealth distribution by percentile

3.name: Education level
3.notes: High school = HS diploma only, college = associate's or bachelor's, graduate = master's+

4.name: Occupation sector
4.notes: Service = service occupations, manual = production/construction, professional = technical/professional, management = management/executive

5.name: Employment stability
5.source: BLS contingent worker supplement and job tenure data
5.notes: Stable = traditional long-term employment, contract = temporary/gig, unstable = frequent job changes

6.name: Employment type
6.source: BLS CPS Employment Status Table A-1, March 2026
6.notes: Includes civilian population 16+, gig estimated from supplemental surveys

7.name: Industry
7.notes: Tech includes professional/business services, education, health, tech sectors

8.name: Job seniority
8.source: BLS tenure and age distribution data
8.notes: Approximated from median tenure and age-occupation distributions

11.name: Age group
11.notes: child <15, youth 15-19, young adult 20-34, mid-career 35-54, senior 55+

12.name: Gender identity
12.source: Census population by sex 2024, nonbinary from surveys
12.notes: Nonbinary estimate from Williams Institute and Pew Research

14.name: Race/ethnicity
14.source: Census 2024 - White non-Hispanic vs. all others
14.notes: Majority = White non-Hispanic (~60%), minority = all other groups

15.name: Immigration status
15.source: Census ACS foreign-born population + DHS estimates

17.name: Years since immigration
17.source: Census ACS foreign-born entry year distribution

18.name: Language proficiency
18.source: Census ACS language spoken at home/English proficiency
18.notes: Fluent = English only or very well, intermediate = well, limited = not well/not at all

19.name: Primary language
19.source: Census ACS language spoken at home 2024
19.notes: English only at home = 78%, Spanish 13%, Asian languages ~9%

20.name: Cultural integration
20.source: Derived from immigration duration and language proficiency
20.notes: High = native-born + long-term immigrants, medium = mid-term, low = recent arrivals

21.name: Marital status
21.source: Census CPS Annual Social and Economic Supplement 2024

23.name: Number of children
23.notes: Among all households, not just families with children

26.name: Household income diversity
26.source: BLS CPS - married couple families labor force participation
26.notes: Among couple households, ~55% have both working

27.name: Household stability
27.source: Census mobility rates and housing tenure
27.notes: Stable = same residence >1 year, unstable = frequent moves/housing insecurity

29.name: Family wealth support
29.source: Federal Reserve Survey of Consumer Finances - intergenerational transfers
29.notes: Estimated from inheritance and family financial support patterns

30.name: Intergenerational mobility
30.source: Census economic mobility studies, Chetty et al.

31.name: Physical health
31.notes: Good = excellent/very good/good, moderate = fair, poor = poor

32.name: Mental health
32.notes: Poor = diagnosed conditions/frequent poor mental health days

33.name: Chronic illness
33.notes: ~40% have at least one chronic condition

34.name: Disability status
34.source: Census ACS disability status 2024 - 13% with disability

36.name: Healthcare access
36.source: HRSA health professional shortage area data + access surveys
36.notes: Limited = shortage areas or uninsured, strong = good insurance + nearby facilities

37.name: Healthcare affordability
37.notes: Unaffordable = high out-of-pocket costs or medical debt

38.name: Substance abuse risk
38.source: SAMHSA NSDUH substance use disorder estimates
38.notes: High = substance use disorder, moderate = misuse, low = none/minimal

39.name: Life expectancy risk
39.source: CDC mortality and life expectancy by demographics
39.notes: Based on health conditions, behaviors, socioeconomic factors

40.name: Preventive care usage
40.notes: Regular = annual checkups, screenings as recommended

41.name: Housing status
41.source: Census Housing Vacancy Survey Q4 2025 - 65.9% homeownership
41.notes: Homeless = HUD point-in-time count ~0.2% of population

42.name: Housing stability
42.source: Census residential mobility + eviction lab data
42.notes: Stable = no recent moves, no rent burden >50%, unstable = frequent moves/risk

43.name: Housing affordability
43.source: HUD comprehensive housing affordability strategy - cost burden >30% income

45.name: Neighborhood safety
45.source: FBI UCR crime statistics by area + victimization surveys
45.notes: Based on crime rates and resident perceptions

47.name: Exposure to pollution
47.notes: Air quality index and proximity to pollution sources

48.name: Access to green space
48.source: Trust for Public Land ParkScore + Census urbanization
48.notes: Proximity to parks and recreational areas

49.name: Disaster risk exposure
49.notes: Flood, fire, hurricane, earthquake, tornado risk combined

50.name: Infrastructure quality
50.source: ASCE infrastructure report card + local assessments
50.notes: Roads, bridges, utilities, public services quality

51.name: Geography type
51.notes: Urban = core cities, suburban = metro areas outside cores, rural = nonmetro

53.name: Region economic growth
53.source: BEA regional economic accounts, employment trends
53.notes: Based on GDP growth, employment, population trends

54.name: Transportation access
54.source: Census transportation statistics + ACS commute data
54.notes: Vehicle ownership, transit availability, road infrastructure

55.name: Commute time
55.source: Census ACS commute time to work

56.name: Access to public transit
56.notes: None = no service, limited = bus only, extensive = rail/frequent service

58.name: Internet access
58.source: NTIA Internet Use Survey Nov 2023 + FCC broadband data
58.notes: None = no home internet, slow = dial-up/satellite/limited mobile, high-speed = cable/fiber

59.name: Local job availability
59.notes: Based on unemployment rate and job openings ratio

61.name: Access to quality schools
61.source: NCES school performance and ED Department data
61.notes: Based on test scores, graduation rates, resources

64.name: Legal representation access
64.source: Legal Services Corporation justice gap reports
64.notes: None = can't afford, limited = pro bono/legal aid, reliable = can hire

65.name: Access to social services
65.source: HHS facility locator + SNAP/TANF participation data
65.notes: Food banks, housing assistance, health services availability

66.name: Banking access
66.source: FDIC National Survey of Unbanked and Underbanked Households 2023
66.notes: Unbanked = no account, basic = checking only, full = checking+savings+credit

67.name: Credit access
67.source: CFPB credit bureau data and FICO score distributions

68.name: Political representation
68.source: Census voter registration and turnout, representation studies
68.notes: Based on voting power, district competitiveness, engagement

69.name: Voting access
69.source: EAC Election Administration and Voting Survey
69.notes: Based on registration ease, polling access, wait times

70.name: Government trust
70.source: Pew Research Center trust in government surveys 2024
70.notes: Historical trends show declining trust over decades

71.name: Social network size
71.source: General Social Survey social network module
71.notes: Number of close contacts and social connections

72.name: Community support
72.source: Social Capital Project data, community surveys
72.notes: Neighborhood cohesion, mutual aid, social capital

73.name: Religious community participation
73.notes: None = never attend, occasional = few times/year, active = weekly+

74.name: Civic participation
74.source: Census Current Population Survey civic engagement supplement

76.name: Social isolation
76.source: National Academies Social Isolation report, health surveys
76.notes: High = chronic loneliness, limited social contact

77.name: Informal economic support
77.source: Federal Reserve Economic Well-Being survey - family assistance
77.notes: Financial help from family/friends, shared resources

78.name: Mentorship access
78.source: Education and workforce development program participation
78.notes: Career guidance, role models, professional development

80.name: Community leadership role
80.source: Census civic engagement data, nonprofit participation

81.name: Job security
81.source: BLS job tenure and layoff statistics
81.notes: Based on employment type, industry, tenure

83.name: Legal vulnerability
83.source: Immigration status, criminal justice, civil legal needs
83.notes: High = undocumented/criminal record, low = citizen/clean record

84.name: Exposure to discrimination
84.notes: Based on protected class membership, self-reported experiences

85.name: Exposure to policing
85.source: BJS police contact surveys, arrest statistics
85.notes: Stop rates, arrest rates, neighborhood policing intensity

86.name: Workplace power
86.source: Occupational authority, management levels, union presence

90.name: Financial resilience
90.source: Federal Reserve SHED emergency savings + CFPB financial well-being
90.notes: Low = can't cover $400 emergency, high = 3+ months expenses saved

91.name: Digital literacy
91.source: PIAAC digital skills assessment, Pew digital divides
91.notes: Ability to use computers, internet, digital tools effectively

92.name: Access to computers
92.source: Census ACS computer and internet use supplement
92.notes: None = no household computer, shared = family device, personal = own device

93.name: Smartphone access
93.notes: Advanced = smartphone, basic = feature phone, none = no mobile

94.name: Online information exposure
94.source: Pew internet use surveys, NTIA digital nation
94.notes: Daily internet use, information seeking behaviors

95.name: Media consumption diversity
95.source: Pew news consumption surveys, media fragmentation studies
95.notes: Low = single source, high = varied sources and perspectives

96.name: Misinformation exposure
96.notes: Vulnerability to and sharing of false information

97.name: Remote work capability
97.source: BLS telework supplement 2024, occupation telework potential
97.notes: Full = job can be done entirely remote, partial = hybrid

98.name: Online professional presence
98.source: LinkedIn member data, professional networking surveys
98.notes: Strong = active LinkedIn/portfolio, basic = minimal presence, none = no presence

99.name: Participation in digital economy
99.source: Census e-commerce participation, gig economy surveys
99.notes: Online selling, gig platforms, digital services

100.name: AI/automation skill exposure
100.source: Burning Glass labor market analytics, AI adoption surveys
100.notes: High = AI tools user/developer, low = minimal exposure
